grammarLessons.name: 내 vs 제 — My (Informal vs Formal)

cards.grammarNote:
Pain is expressed with **아프다** (to hurt/be sick).
The body part takes the subject particle **이/가**: **머리가 아파요** = my head hurts.
This construction is used for all body pain.

**머리카락** (meorikaraк) = hair strand — compound: **머리** (head) + **카락** (strand/piece).
**머리** alone can mean both 'head' and 'hair' in casual speech; **머리카락** is specifically a hair strand.
**머리카락이 길어요** = her hair is long — **이** is the subject particle after consonant-final **락**.
**길다** (gilda) = to be long → **길어요** (gireoyo) in polite speech (ㄹ stem + 어요).

**얼굴** (eolgul) = face — pure Korean noun.
**얼굴을 씻다** = to wash one's face — **씻다** (ssitda) = to wash; **을** is the object particle.
**씻으세요** = please wash — **씻다** + **으세요** (polite imperative after consonant-final stem).
Related: **얼굴색** (eolgulsaek) = facial complexion; **인상** (insang) = facial impression.

**눈** (nun) = eye — pure Korean; also means 'snow' (same spelling, different context).
Korean does not mark grammatical plurality: **눈** = eye or eyes depending on context.
**눈을 감다** = to close one's eyes; **눈을 뜨다** = to open one's eyes.
Related compound: **눈물** (nunmul) = tears (lit. eye-water); **눈썹** (nunsseop) = eyebrow.

**귀** (gwi) = ear — pure Korean noun.
**귀가 아파요** = my ear hurts — **가** is the subject particle after vowel-final **귀**.
Pain expression pattern: body part + **이/가** + **아파요** (from **아프다**, to hurt/be sick).
Related: **귀걸이** (gwigeolli) = earring (lit. ear-hanger); **귀머거리** = deaf person (informal/old).

**코** (ko) = nose — pure Korean noun.
**코가 차가워요** = my nose is cold — **가** is the subject particle; **차갑다** (chagapda) = to be cold (to the touch).
**차갑다** → **차가워요** (chagawoyo) in polite speech — ㅂ irregular.
Related: **코막힘** (komakiм) = nasal congestion (lit. nose-blockage); **콧물** (konmul) = runny nose/mucus.

**입** (ip) = mouth — pure Korean noun.
**입을 벌리다** = to open one's mouth — **벌리다** (beollida) = to spread apart/open wide.
**벌리세요** = please open — **벌리다** + **세요** (polite imperative after vowel-final stem).
Related: **입술** (ipsul) = lips; **입맛** (immат) = taste/appetite (lit. mouth-taste).

**입술** (ipsul) = lip/lips — compound: **입** (ip, mouth) + **술** (old word for edge/border).
Korean uses the same word **입술** for one lip or both lips — number is inferred from context.
**빨갛다** (ppalgatda) = to be red → **빨개요** (ppalg aeyo) in polite speech — ㅎ irregular.
**이** after **입술** is the subject particle (consonant-final **술**).

**이** (i) = tooth/teeth — pure Korean; same as the subject particle **이**, so context is key.
**이가 아파요** = I have a toothache (lit. 'tooth hurts') — the subject particle **가** follows vowel-final **이**.
Alternative word: **치아** (chia, 齒牙) — Sino-Korean, used in formal/medical contexts (e.g. at the dentist).
Related: **이를 닦다** (ireul dakda) = to brush one's teeth; **충치** (chungchi) = cavity (Sino-Korean).

**혀** (hyeo) = tongue — pure Korean noun.
**혀를 내밀다** = to stick out one's tongue — **내밀다** (naemilda) = to extend/stick out.
**내미세요** = please stick out — polite imperative; **를** is the object particle after vowel-final **혀**.
Related: **혓바늘** (hyeotbaneul) = tongue sore/ulcer; **혀가 꼬이다** = to get tongue-tied.

**목** (mok) = neck — pure Korean; also means 'throat' in some contexts.
**목이 뻣뻣해요** = my neck is stiff — **이** is the subject particle; **뻣뻣하다** = to be stiff/rigid.
**목소리** (moksori) = voice — compound: **목** (throat) + **소리** (sound).
For throat pain: **목이 아파요** = my throat hurts; **목감기** (mokkamgi) = throat cold.

**팔** (pal) = arm — pure Korean noun (also means 'eight' — a homonym).
**팔이 골절됐어요** = I broke my arm — **골절** (goljeol, 骨折) = fracture; Sino-Korean.
**골절되다** (goljeol-doeda) = to be fractured — **되다** makes it passive; past: **됐어요**.
Related: **팔꿈치** (palkkumchi) = elbow; **오른팔** (oreunpal) = right arm (compound).

**손** (son) = hand/hands — pure Korean; no plural marking needed in Korean.
**손을 씻다** = to wash one's hands — **씻다** (ssitda) = to wash; **을** is the object particle.
Compounds: **손가락** (songarak) = finger (hand-branch); **손목** (sonmok) = wrist (hand-neck).
Related: **악수하다** (aksuhada) = to shake hands — Sino-Korean 握手.

**손가락** (songarak) = finger — compound: **손** (son, hand) + **가락** (garak, long slender object).
Toe is **발가락** (balgarak): **발** (bal, foot) + **가락** — parallel compound structure.
**베다** (beda) = to cut/slice; **베었어요** = cut (past tense) — **베다** + **었어요**.
Finger joints: **손가락 마디** (songarak madi); fingernail: **손톱** (sontop, hand-shell).

**가슴** (gaseum) = chest — pure Korean; covers both chest and breast/heart area emotionally.
**가슴이 아파요** = chest hurts — **이** is the subject particle after consonant-final **슴**.
**가슴이 뛰다** = heart is beating/chest is pounding — idiomatic for excitement or fear.
Medical term for chest: **흉부** (hyungbu, 胸部) — Sino-Korean used in clinical settings.

**등** (deung) = back (of the body) — pure Korean noun.
**등이 아파요** = my back hurts — **이** is the subject particle after consonant-final **등**.
Lower back is **허리** (heori); **등** refers specifically to the upper/mid back.
Related: **등뼈** (deungppyeo) = spine (lit. back-bone); **척추** (cheokchu) = spine (Sino-Korean medical term).

**심장** (simjang) is Sino-Korean: 心臟 — the physical heart organ.
For emotional 'heart', use **마음** (maeum) = heart/mind (pure Korean).
**심장이 뛰다** = heart is beating — **뛰다** (ttwida) = to beat/jump/run.
**빨리** (ppalli) = quickly/fast — common adverb; **심장이 빨리 뛰어요** = heart is beating fast.

**배** (bae) = stomach/belly/abdomen — pure Korean; also means 'ship/boat' and 'pear' (homonyms).
**배가 아파요** = my stomach hurts — **가** is the subject particle after vowel-final **배**.
The internal organ (stomach) is **위** (wi, 胃) in medical/Sino-Korean; **배** is the colloquial term.
Related: **배가 고프다** (bae-ga gopda) = to be hungry (lit. stomach is empty).

**근육** (geunyuk) is Sino-Korean: 筋肉 — muscle/musculature.
**근육을 다치다** = to injure a muscle — **다치다** (dachida) = to get hurt/injured.
**다쳤어요** = was injured (past tense): **다치다** + **었어요** → **다쳤어요**.
Related: **근육통** (geunyuktong) = muscle pain/soreness; **통** (通/痛) = pain/ache (Sino-Korean suffix).

**허리** (heori) = waist/lower back — pure Korean noun.
**허리가 가늘다** = waist is slim — **가늘다** (ganeulda) = to be thin/slender → **가늘어요**.
**허리가 아파요** = my lower back hurts — **허리** also refers to the lower back area.
Related: **허리띠** (heoriddi) = belt (lit. waist-band); **요통** (yotong) = lower back pain (Sino-Korean medical term).

**피부** (pibu) is Sino-Korean: 皮膚 — skin (the standard word in both everyday and medical use).
**피부가 부드럽다** = skin is soft — **부드럽다** (budeureopda) = to be soft → **부드러워요** (ㅂ irregular).
**피부과** (pibugwa) = dermatology clinic — **과** (科) = department/specialty (Sino-Korean).
Related: **피부색** (pibusaek) = skin color/complexion; **살** (sal) = flesh/skin (pure Korean, informal).

**피** (pi) = blood — pure Korean noun.
The medical/formal term is **혈액** (hyeolaek, 血液) — Sino-Korean, used in clinical contexts.
**헌혈하다** (heonhyeolhada) = to donate blood — **헌** (heon, 獻) = to donate; **혈** (hyeol, 血) = blood.
**피가 나다** = blood comes out (to bleed); **피를 흘리다** = to shed blood.

**뇌** (noe) is Sino-Korean: 腦 — brain (both everyday and medical usage).
**뇌는 복잡해요** = the brain is complex — **는** is the topic particle after vowel-final **뇌**.
**복잡하다** (bokjapada) = to be complex/complicated → **복잡해요**.
Related: **뇌졸중** (noejoljung) = stroke; **뇌파** (noepa) = brain waves — common compound words.

**두개골** (dugaegol) is Sino-Korean: 頭蓋骨 — skull; lit. 'head-cover-bone'.
Breakdown: **두** (頭, head) + **개** (蓋, cover/lid) + **골** (骨, bone).
**보호하다** (bohohada) = to protect — **보호** (保護) is Sino-Korean; **해요** form in polite speech.
**뼈** (ppyeo) = bone (pure Korean); **골** (骨) = bone only in Sino-Korean compound words.

**가슴** (gaseum) covers both chest and breast — context determines the specific meaning.
**통증** (tongjeung, 痛症) = pain/ache — Sino-Korean medical term; more clinical than **아픔**.
**통증을 느끼다** = to feel pain — **느끼다** (neukkida) = to feel/sense; **을** is the object particle.
**느꼈어요** = felt (past tense): **느끼다** + **었어요** → **느꼈어요** (ㅣ + 어 contracts).
cards.englishPhrase: She felt pain in her chest.

cards.grammarNote:
**다리** (dari) = leg — pure Korean noun; also means 'bridge' (same spelling, different context).
**다리가 피곤해요** = my legs are tired — **가** is the subject particle after vowel-final **다리**.
**피곤하다** (pigonhada) = to be tired/fatigued — Sino-Korean 疲困 → **피곤해요**.
Related: **다리뼈** (darippyeo) = leg bone; **무릎** (mureup) = knee; **발** (bal) = foot.

**발** (bal) = foot/feet — pure Korean; no separate plural form in Korean.
**발이 아파요** = my feet hurt — **이** is the subject particle after consonant-final **발**.
Compounds: **발가락** (balgarak) = toe (foot-branch); **발목** (balmok) = ankle (foot-neck).
Related: **맨발** (maenbal) = bare feet (lit. plain-feet); **발바닥** (balbadak) = sole of the foot.

**발가락** (balgarak) = toe — compound: **발** (bal, foot) + **가락** (garak, slender appendage).
Parallel to **손가락** (songarak, finger): the **가락** root applies to both fingers and toes.
**부딪히다** (budichiда) = to bump into/stub — passive form of **부딪다** (to bump).
**발톱** (baltop) = toenail (lit. foot-claw); **손톱** (sontop) = fingernail (lit. hand-claw).

**관절** (gwanjeol) is Sino-Korean: 關節 — joint (where bones connect).
**관절이 뻣뻣해요** = my joints are stiff — **이** is the subject particle; **뻣뻣하다** = to be rigid/stiff.
Related: **관절염** (gwanjeoryeom) = arthritis — **염** (炎) = inflammation (common medical suffix).
**연골** (yeongol) = cartilage (Sino-Korean: 軟骨 = soft-bone); **인대** (indae) = ligament.

**숨** (sum) = breath — pure Korean noun.
**숨을 참다** = to hold one's breath — **참다** (chamda) = to endure/hold back.
**참으세요** = please hold — **참다** + **으세요** (polite imperative after consonant-final **참**).
Related: **숨을 쉬다** = to breathe; **호흡** (hohup, 呼吸) = breathing/respiration (Sino-Korean medical term).

**보다** (boda) = to see/look/watch — pure Korean verb.
**보이다** (boida) = to be visible/to appear — passive/spontaneous form of **보다**.
The phrase **선명하게 보여요** = can be seen clearly — **보여요** is the polite form of **보이다**.
**선명하게** (seonmyeonghage) = clearly — adverb from **선명하다** (to be clear/vivid).

**듣다** (deutda) = to hear/listen — pure Korean verb with ㄷ irregular.
**듣다** → **들어요** (deureoyo) in polite speech — ㄷ changes to ㄹ before vowels.
Passive/spontaneous: **들리다** (deullida) = to be heard/audible → **들려요** (deullyeoyo).
**들리지 않아요** = cannot be heard — **-지 않다** is the standard negation pattern.

**냄새** (naemse) = smell/scent — pure Korean noun; **맡다** (matda) = to smell (actively sniff).
The phrase uses **냄새가 나다** = a smell comes out — spontaneous sense perception (passive).
**꽃 냄새가 나요** = the flower smell comes/there is a flower scent.
Related: **향기** (hyanggi) = fragrance/aroma (pleasant smell); **악취** (akchwi) = bad smell/stench.

**맛보다** (matboda) = to taste — compound: **맛** (mat, taste) + **보다** (to see/try).
**맛보세요** = please taste — polite imperative: **맛보** + **세요**.
**맛** alone = flavor/taste (noun); **맛있다** (masitda) = to be delicious; **맛없다** = to taste bad.
Related: **미각** (migak, 味覺) = the sense of taste — Sino-Korean medical/formal term.

**만지다** (manjida) = to touch/handle — pure Korean verb.
**만지지 마세요** = please don't touch — **-지 마세요** is the polite negative imperative.
**만지다** vs **닿다** (datda): **만지다** = to touch with intent; **닿다** = to come into contact with.
Related: **촉각** (chokgak, 觸覺) = the sense of touch — Sino-Korean; **감촉** (gamchok) = texture/feel.

**숨을 쉬다** (sumeul swida) = to breathe — lit. 'to breathe/rest one's breath'.
**숨** (sum) = breath (noun); **쉬다** (swida) = to rest/breathe; **을** is the object particle.
**쉬세요** = please breathe — **쉬다** + **세요** (polite imperative after vowel-final stem).
Alternative: **호흡하다** (hohupada) = to breathe (Sino-Korean, more formal/medical).

**구부리다** (gubeurida) = to bend/curve — pure Korean verb.
**구부리세요** = please bend — polite imperative: stem **구부리** + **세요**.
**무릎을 구부리다** = to bend one's knees — **을** is the object particle after **무릎**.
Related: **구부러지다** (gubureojida) = to be bent/curved (intransitive/passive form).

**늘이다** (neurida) = to stretch/lengthen — pure Korean verb (transitive).
The phrase uses **뻗다** (ppeotda) = to extend/stretch out — more natural for extending limbs.
**팔을 쭉 뻗으세요** = please stretch your arms straight — **쭉** = fully/straight (adverb).
**뻗으세요** = please stretch — **뻗다** + **으세요** (polite imperative after consonant-final stem).

**어깨** (eokkae) = shoulder — pure Korean noun.
**어깨가 아파요** = my shoulder hurts — **가** is the subject particle after vowel-final **어깨**.
Idiom: **어깨가 무겁다** = to feel a heavy burden (lit. shoulders are heavy) — figurative use.
Related: **어깨동무** (eokkaedongmu) = putting arms around each other's shoulders (friendship gesture).

**무릎** (mureup) = knee — pure Korean noun.
**무릎을 다치다** = to hurt one's knee — **다치다** (dachida) = to get hurt; **을** is the object particle.
**무릎을 꿇다** (mureupеul kkultda) = to kneel (lit. to bend the knee) — a common idiom.
Related: **무릎뼈** (mureupppyeo) = kneecap; **슬개골** (seulgaegol) = patella (Sino-Korean medical term).

**팔꿈치** (palkkumchi) = elbow — compound: **팔** (pal, arm) + **꿈치** (kkumchi, bent tip/end).
Parallel to **발꿈치** (balkkumchi) = heel — **꿈치** is the pure Korean word for a protrusion/tip.
**팔꿈치가 아파요** = my elbow hurts — **가** is the subject particle after vowel-final **치**.
Related: **팔씨름** (palssireum) = arm wrestling; **팔꿈치를 짚다** = to prop oneself on one's elbow.

**근육을 긴장시키다** = to flex muscles — lit. 'to tense the muscles'.
**긴장시키다** = causative: **긴장** (tension) + **시키다** (to cause/make do).
The phrase uses **근육에 힘을 주다** = to put strength into muscles — more natural in speech.
**힘을 주다** = to exert force/flex — **힘** (him) = strength/force; **주다** = to give.

**눈을 깜빡이다** = to blink — lit. 'to flicker one's eyes'.
**깜빡이다** (kkamppagida) = to blink/flicker — pure Korean verb (reduplicated sound-imitative root).
**눈을 깜빡이지 마세요** = please don't blink — **-지 마세요** is the polite negative imperative.
Related: **깜빡** (kkamppak) = flash/blink (onomatopoeia); **눈을 깜빡하다** = informal variant.

**땀을 흘리다** = to sweat — lit. 'to shed/flow sweat'; **땀** (ttam) = sweat; **흘리다** = to spill/shed.
**흘리다** is used for liquids flowing: **눈물을 흘리다** = to shed tears; **피를 흘리다** = to shed blood.
**달릴 때** = when running — **-ㄹ/을 때** expresses 'when [doing something]'.
Related: **땀띠** (ttamddi) = heat rash; **식은땀** (sigeun-ttam) = cold sweat.

**피가 나다** = to bleed — lit. 'blood comes out'; **나다** (nada) = to emerge/come out.
**가** is the subject particle after vowel-final **피**: **피가 나요** = blood is coming out.
**상처** (sangcheo, 傷處) = wound — Sino-Korean; **에서** = from (location of origin).
Related: **출혈** (chulhyeol, 出血) = bleeding/hemorrhage — Sino-Korean medical term.

**느끼다** (neukkida) = to feel/sense — pure Korean verb.
**기분이 어때요?** = How do you feel? — lit. 'how is your mood?' — uses **기분** + **이/가** + state question.
**어때요** (eottaeyo) = how is it? — from **어떻다** (eotteohda), a common question form.
Related: **느낌** (neukkum) = feeling/sense/impression (noun form of **느끼다**).

**생각하다** (saenggakhada) = to think — **생각** (saenggak, thought/idea) + **하다**.
**생각하고 있어요** = is thinking — **-고 있다** is the progressive pattern (to be doing).
**무슨 생각** = what thought/what are you thinking — **무슨** (museun) = what kind of.
Related: **생각나다** (saenggaknada) = to come to mind; **생각하다** vs **생각나다** is active vs. spontaneous.

**움직이다** (umjigida) = to move — pure Korean verb.
**팔을 움직일 수 없어요** = I can't move my arm — **-ㄹ/을 수 없다** = cannot (lit. 'there is no way to').
**움직이다** → **움직여요** (umjigyeoyo) in polite speech.
Related: **움직임** (umjigim) = movement (noun); **꼼짝도 못하다** = cannot move at all (idiomatic).

**뼈가 앙상한** = bony — lit. 'bones are gaunt/bare'; **앙상하다** = to be gaunt/bony.
**뼈** (ppyeo) = bone — pure Korean; medical term is **골** (骨, gol) in Sino-Korean compounds.
**앙상하다** → **앙상해요** (angsanghaeyo) in polite speech.
The phrase structure **의 손이 앙상해요** = possessive **의** + body part + state adjective.

**털이 많은** = hairy — lit. 'having many hairs'; **털** (teol) = body hair/fur/feather.
**털** is the pure Korean word for body/animal hair; **머리카락** is specifically head hair.
**많다** (manta) = to be many/much → modifier form **많은** (manheun) before nouns.
**팔에 털이 많아요** = has a lot of hair on the arms — **에** marks the location (on the arms).

**부드러운** (budeuroun) is the modifier form of **부드럽다** (budeureopda, to be soft) — ㅂ irregular.
**부드럽다** → **부드러워요** (budeureowoyo) in polite speech (ㅂ → 워 before vowels).
**티슈** (tisyu) = tissue — loanword from English; **가** is the subject particle after vowel-final **슈**.
Related: **말랑하다** (mallangada) = to be soft/squishy (for squishy textures); **촉촉하다** = to be moist.

**아픈** (apeun) is the modifier form of **아프다** (apeuda, to hurt/be sick) — ㅡ irregular.
**아프다** → **아파요** (apayo) in polite speech (ㅡ drops before 아).
The phrase uses **쑤시다** (ssusida) = to ache/throb — specifically for muscle soreness or dull aching pain.
**근육이 쑤셔요** = muscles ache/are sore — a more specific term than the general **아파요**.

**왼쪽** (oenjjok) = left side — compound: **왼** (oen, left) + **쪽** (jjok, side/direction).
**왼** alone means left; adding **쪽** specifies direction: **왼쪽으로** = to the left.
**왼쪽 무릎** = left knee — **왼쪽** directly modifies the body part as an adjective.
Opposite: **오른쪽** (oreunjjok) = right side; **왼손** (oenson) = left hand; **오른손** = right hand.

**오른쪽** (oreunjjok) = right side — compound: **오른** (oreun, right) + **쪽** (jjok, side/direction).
**오른팔** (oreunpal) = right arm — **오른** directly combines with the body part (no 쪽 needed).
**골절되다** (goljeol-doeda) = to be fractured — **골절** (骨折) is Sino-Korean; **되다** = to become.
**됐어요** = past tense of **되다**: **되다** + **었어요** → **됐어요** (contracted form).

**상체** (sangche) is Sino-Korean: 上體 — upper body; lit. 'upper-body'.
**상** (上) = upper/above; **체** (體) = body — common in anatomical compounds.
Opposite: **하체** (hache, 下體) = lower body; **전신** (jeonsin) = whole body.
**강하다** (ganghada) = to be strong — **강해요** (ganghaeyo) in polite speech.

**하체** (hache) is Sino-Korean: 下體 — lower body; **하** (下) = lower/below; **체** (體) = body.
The phrase uses **허리가 아파요** for lower back pain — **허리** is more specific than **하체**.
**요통** (yotong, 腰痛) = lower back pain — Sino-Korean medical term: **요** (腰, waist) + **통** (痛, pain).
Related: **하체 운동** (hache undong) = lower body exercise; **다리** and **발** are parts of **하체**.

**몸** (mom) = body — pure Korean noun; used in everyday speech.
The phrase uses **인체** (inche, 人體) = human body — Sino-Korean, more formal/scientific.
**몸이 좋다/나쁘다** = to feel well/unwell (lit. body is good/bad) — common health expression.
Related: **몸무게** (mommuge) = body weight; **몸살** (momsal) = body ache (lit. body-flesh pain).

**인간** (ingan) is Sino-Korean: 人間 — human being; **의** (ui) = possessive/genitive particle.
**인간의** = human's/of human — used attributively before nouns: **인간의 몸** = the human body.
Alternative: **인체** (inche) = the human body; **인류** (illyu) = humankind/humanity.
**생물학** (saengmulhak) = biology — Sino-Korean: 生物學; **의** links it to **인간**.
